channel.title: Comments for page "I Was Not Magnificent"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "I Was Not Magnificent"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1, definitely a fun read. I'm not the biggest fan of all the crosslinks, but the tale itself was entertaining and well-written enough.

Honestly, while I was reading, I couldn't help but feel a fairytale/children's book vibe. I'm not sure how to describe it. The feeling was especially strong in the riding Gregory and the "Crack, crunch, crumble" parts of the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You had me at

"I do. And don't call me Shirley."

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The sacred texts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In the garage with the ceiling falling Roget comes into the backseat (also how can a simulation element come through another simulation element the simulation's supposed to treat like all of it is real things is it because it's breaking down and the simulation is forgetting things are there and not realizing things properly?), but after that the spiders overwhelm him and he is outside but he never went outside and I think he'd probably be too scared to leave in the middle of ceiling falling and spider attacks but both being overwhelmed by spiders and coming to Gillespie in the car because he's scared are really nice bits of the story so I told you user Roget because I didn't want to edit the intended way things were and I also had no idea how to keep both those parts and justify his moving I guess if it came down to it I'd prefer keeping Roget running to hide in the car with his Gramma maybe it takes away the cute little kid sorta vibe when he gets swallowed away by the spiders and he is gone if there's nobody who takes notice of it and it moves on to the next parts but coming to her and wailing is nice if you pick one to keep and don't find a way to tie both bits in the story I got no idea how to do that but you might :/ and it was a good story I liked it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I only recently read the tale because it's only recently got translated into the Chinese site, and tbh, it did not leave me with a good impression. Not only that but it competely destoryed my previously good impression of the character from SCP-2322.

It seems to be just a wacky adventure of the grandma site director with Dr. Light into a skip where they conveniently bumped into a bunch of other skips in a lolfoundation fashion without actually being funny. And it in an awkward attempt tries to establish their character in conversations and details, but it only feels to me like trying to write after the doctrines of some writing guide. Reaing it, I do not feel for the characters at all. While in the end there's some deeper plot I was not aware of revealed, but I don't actually understand what happened, and it seems like just trying to play clever and mysterious.

Generally, -1.

Are you happy now rumetzen, are you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
""Shirley, you never know what's out there."

"I do. And don't call me Shirley.""
Aaaand upvoted. Also I liked some of the crosslinks, though a couple of them didn't seem to have aged well iirc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Congratulation, you now bear the mark of the nerd. Forever will you be enslaved to Roget and his interest in hockey and antiquated consumer electronic goods. May god have mercy on your soul.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cool, I was getting a little worried since it's been almost a month since resurrection has been updated. I was spoiled with a ton of new content near the beginning I guess.

Also: Roget responded to me aaaaaaaa

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: more detail on that is forthcoming

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You used an interrobang +20

But seriously, I thought this was good and it reintroduced me to a bunch of cool SCPs I had mostly forgotten about. I do wish there was more about how the automaton got out in the first place. Light mentions that since she joined the alpha-9 program she was told to wear a bulletproof vest, the GOC is actively trying to kill members of alpha-9 in Clef's girls night out thing, so I was expecting a connection there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this your way of hinting that your going to try to implement an instance of 1513 into Alpha-9?? I honestly think that'd be pretty cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was actually really close to cutting that a few times, but it was difficult for me because I just loved the imagery too much. We're definitely going to be meeting Gregory II eventually, maybe, possibly, hopefully. Maybe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, I like a good story, especially long ones that are entertaining throughout . I was smiling like an idiot imagining the giant Doberman thing charging down the halls with its terrified passengers clinging to it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
FWIW, almost every time I've heard this referred to, it's just been "bromance" regardless of gender.

(Not that this is necessarily awesome, but it's what I've seen~)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dunno, dude. Maybe it can turn into more than friendship, and it would be pretty cool if it did, but at this point they've literally known each other for like a day, for most of which they were concerned with survival.

Maybe it's qualitatively different than friendships that weren't forged in fire, so to speak, but I don't see how there is or could be anything romantic about it at this point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it was just friendship, we wouldn't call it a bromance, now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neat, I'd never seen that one before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-1837

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The word you're looking for is "friendship".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. This could have been three tales easily, but it didn't feel long. I got excited when you brought 2322, and I like the, uh, whatever you call a bromance when both parties are women. What the heck is the mop though?

I got your back on some typos, except I'm not sure about this line:

The checkpoint guard to their left opened

The entire guard opened and gibberish spilled out? He opened his mouth? The door? Also, there are some other sections that didn't quite feel on board, but nothing easy to pinpoint.

item.title: minor grammar thing
item.content:encoded:
I imagine those should all be too not to

to tired, to sleepy, to asleep

EDIT: just went ahead and changed it myself

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
considering the people and the ambition behind this series there needs to be a higher level of execution than this.

If it makes you feel better, we edit Resurrection works more extensively than almost anything else on the wiki, so we're at least trying for a higher level of execution. =] Even if it didn't work for you here, which is fine.

I agree that the crosslink sections could be fleshed out a little — I would suggest that Roget go through and just add a bit here and there, maybe some conversation or thoughts from the characters' POVs about each SCPs.

The crosslinks still work for me, though (although, no one suggested the crosslink flood except Roget.)

The rest, Roget would have to answer~

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two (ostensible) errors I found:

carrying them. on its back

The air was think

But still, fantastic story, absolutely. This line:

And don't call me Shirley.

made me laugh. I hope that was intended. Besides a captivating premise and great use of various SCPs, this was very well written, although there was a point (I believe when they were riding Gregory) that I wasn't entirely sure what exactly was going on.

As well, they got out of mirror-77 and went to reminisce? Didn't they talk about the possibility of subterfuge in how charito-tron was able to escape containment? That seems like a pressing concern, but they don't touch on it.

All in all, I enjoyed the piece, as I do with almost all of your writing. +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an exciting story and a good use of characters. One criticism would be that in the middle section where they're being chased, the action is hard to follow. The sequence with the robot busting through 031 and 1636 still doesn't make any sense. While I agree that the SCP links threw me out of the story, I think you need them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ahhhhhh I have to give this a -1 as well, at this point, considering the people and the ambition behind this series there needs to be a higher level of execution than this.

It is a rare story that works with a dozen crosslinks, and this one isn't it. At least half of the SCPs could have been swapped with any number of other randomly selected SCPs and the story would be almost identical, and the ones that move the plot don't feel like they were selected for any reason more thoughtful than that they were first one that popped into the authors head when he was looking for McGuffin which could plausibly cause X event. You could rewrite this story a dozen times with a different set of SCPs each and every time and it could be kept nearly identical, that is a sign that these crosslinks didn't work.

Casual crosslinking does nothing but kill the pace of the story, instead of humming along through a really cool idea (Shirley in 2322!) I had to open a new tab every minute to remind myself what the SCP d'jour was, only to have it exit the story not two paragraphs later. If you dropped 6-8 of the crosslinks and just left them in as easter eggs (literally just get rid of the link) I think this story would move a lot better, but even then, this still has a weak opening, characters I still can't get myself to care about, and an underutilization of what should have been a great idea.

If this story had been written outside of the context of Resurrection I think it could have been so much better. Light being in this didn't add anything at all (it doesn't help that I find Light to be one of the most boring characters in this entire series so far), and I suspect the number of crosslinks was motivated at least partially by trying to fufilled the goal of Resurrection re-popularizing them. I just kept getting the feeling like this story was torn in two directions, telling a serious story about Shirley in 2322, or a Resurrection tale. The levity in the story also missed the mark a bit as well, again, it wasn't casual or serious enough, but in a weird middle ground where all the off-beat events, concepts, and phrasings that should normally be charming just felt awkward.

Is this bad? No, it isn't, but it did not draw me in, and it left me with a very real feeling that it missed the mark over and over again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I gave this one a downvote, and I feel like I should explain why I don't like what is probably going to be a well regarded tale.

The problem I have here is that this didn't make me feel anything. There's lots of crosslinked SCPs that are linked back to the original articles (which is a nice touch btw), but left me sort of looking at it like it felt a bit obvious? Like the presence of linked SCPs we recognise was sort of the impact, rather than what they do. This was especially true for me with the spider near the end. It just felt like another piece of work was being dangled in front of me in the hope that would elicit a reaction, and that dangling felt obvious to me while I was reading.

I found what was happening to be extremely hard to follow on first reading. It's obviously packed with action and stuff and SCPs, but that meant I spent the majority of the piece feeling more bewildered than entertained. This meant that the length of the piece started to drag for me, where I've read SCP articles that are possibly longer or in the same area and not experienced that.

By the end, I was rather confused, and I walked away from the piece feeling like something had happened, but I wasn't sure what. Other than that though, the piece was written in a way that was easy to read, and technically it's more than good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy hell, this was great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, it's finally exist. Probably the longest time I've ever spent solidly working on one tale.

Special thanks to thedeadlymoose, clef, light, lorddeath, and anyone else who looked this over as it was being written. I think this is the longest tale I've written for the site, or really the longest piece of fiction I've written in general. I hope you guys enjoy reading it as much as I enjoyed making it.
